topic: job applications — comparing community clinics in Bristol

turns.content: alright so ive been looking at physio jobs and theres a few community clinics in bristol that are hiring but i cant figure out which ones are actually good to work at vs which ones are just putting up a nice website you know

That's a smart thing to be thinking about — a nice website doesn't always mean a good workplace. What kind of things matter most to you in a clinic? Things like caseload, supervision, patient population, pay band?

patient population is massive for me. i want somewhere thats actually serving the community not just doing private knee rehab for middle aged runners. no disrespect to them but thats not why i got into this you know. and supervision matters because im going to be newly qualified so i dont want to be chucked in the deep end with zero support

That makes sense. For community clinics specifically, a few things to look at:

- **Patient demographics** — ask in the interview what their typical patient base looks like. Are they seeing a mix of ages, backgrounds, conditions?
- **Preceptorship or mentoring** — good clinics will have a structured programme for newly qualified physios. Ask about it directly.
- **Caseload expectations** — some places overload new staff. Ask how many patients per day you'd be expected to see in your first 3-6 months.
- **Multi-disciplinary working** — community clinics that work alongside GPs, social prescribers, and mental health teams tend to offer a richer experience.

Are there specific clinics you're comparing?

theres like 3 that ive seen advertised. ones more of a social enterprise type thing which sounds sick but the pay might be lower. another ones nhs band 5 which is standard but the reviews online are mixed. and the third one is a newer place that does like community outreach and pop-up clinics which sounds proper interesting but i dont know how stable it is

Each has trade-offs:

- **Social enterprise** — often more aligned with your values, but you're right that pay can be lower and funding can be grant-dependent. Ask how they're funded and how secure the role is.
- **NHS Band 5** — most stable option, clear pay structure, pension, progression pathway. Mixed reviews could mean anything — high turnover, management issues, or just a few disgruntled people. Worth trying to speak to someone who works there.
- **Community outreach / pop-ups** — sounds exciting and innovative, but newer organisations can be less stable. Ask about their funding model and whether the role is permanent or fixed-term.

Have you applied to all three or still deciding?

still deciding. i feel like the outreach one is the most me but the nhs one is the sensible choice init. like my nan would say take the nhs job Marcus stop being silly haha. but i didnt do 3 years of this to play it safe and sit in a clinic doing the same 5 exercises on repeat

Ha, nan wisdom vs career passion — classic dilemma. But here's the thing: you don't have to commit to one path forever. You could apply to all three and see who actually offers, then make a decision with real options on the table rather than hypotheticals. An interview at the outreach clinic will tell you way more than their website. And if the NHS role turns out to have a community focus and good supervision, it might surprise you.

tbh thats facts. i should just apply to all of them and see what happens. worst case i get interview practice. i just need to update my CV because its looking rough ngl. like i havent touched it since before final year started

Get that CV updated with your most recent clinical experience and any relevant skills — manual therapy, exercise prescription, patient assessment. Tailor it slightly for each application if you can. Community-focused roles will want to see evidence that you understand health inequalities and working with diverse populations. If you need a hand with the CV or a cover letter, happy to help with that too.

yeah i might take you up on that actually. let me get the CV drafted first and then ill come back. appreciate the help tho this has cleared my head a bit. apply to all 3 and stop overthinking it basically haha

Exactly — overthinking is the enemy right now. Get the applications out there and let the process tell you what's right. You clearly know what you want and why, so that'll come through in the interviews. Come back anytime with the CV and we'll tighten it up. Good luck with it.
